original: The Shieldwall Staff: strong enough for a military, but made for a civilian. Most modern armies dump their development budgets into bigger guns, better shields, and better vehicles, viewing melee weapons as tools of last resort, unworthy of significant investment. For smaller and lighter creatures like rodenians, this is even more true. Where others saw the combat’s distant past, KihaCorp saw opportunity. By integrating modern shield projection technology into a bludgeoning staff, they transformed a melee armament into a defensive guard and tool at all the same time. The military version was capable of being planted in the ground via a sharpened tip and interlinked with other nearby Shieldwall staves to create exactly what its name implies: a defensive barrier strong enough to block rail-toting snipers, deflect cannon shells, and reportedly even nullify a thraggen tank’s main laser cannon, though the veracity of that report remains a hotly contested subject of debate on the extranet. The civilian version lacks the multi-staff interlink capability, instead slaving its emitters to boost the owner’s own dedicated shield-projection belt. It has sold extremely well in the leadup to the latest Planet Rush with would-be adventurers wisely prioritizing their own defense - and the comfort of a weapon they can use as a walking stick.

original: “Well, I sought permission first.” Gene harrumphs as he turns and click-thud-clack-thumps his way around the oak-colored space, tails flicking, continuing to talk as he slides fastidious cybernetic fingers over cases and posters. “Discretely. After the kui-tan gave me permission to land and trade here, I approached a couple of gold myr civil servants about room in Gildenmere where I might safely build. It turned out there was quite a lot of that - vacant lots do have a tendency to spring up during the prosecution of lost wars. And they were quite happy, desperate even, to get fresh commerce in, particularly from the stars. Of course, I did not tell them about the nature of autossemblers. Nor that I was going to activate it during rush hour.”

original: “Imagine it,” he insists. “Put yourself in the shoes of your average gold myr. Worn and fraught by conflict, you and your people have been given a stay of execution by the arrival of extraterrestrial beings. Who are they, these mysterious, benevolent beings, what do they want, where, how...? And then, one morning.” He lowers his voice to a ominous hush, his cyber arms opening to the sky. “You are walking down the street to your place of work when a piece of technology of unknowable, wondrous design unfurls itself like a ceramic flower where a part of your shattered city once stood. In mere minutes, with impossible grace and speed, it unfolds piece by piece into a shining white building the likes of which you have never seen. Then - after the clouds have dissipated, and one or two more minutes have dragged by - ” One metal arm points to a corner. If you’re not very much mistaken, a dry ice machine is standing over there. “I emerge on the roof.” He unfolds his massive bat wings to their full extent, eyes fixed on the middle distance.

original: “People of Gildenmere!” he roars, full-throated, making the room shake. You suddenly feel thrilled in a diffuse, indefinable way and perhaps, just for a moment, you are that gold myr, standing frozen in the street in awe. “For too long you have struggled under the yoke of war that is not your path. I come in peace, and to you I bring the fruits of a peace beyond your world that has lasted centuries beyond reckoning. Those brave enough to taste of them can become anything they wish - may break away from the cruel bonds of nature. The only limit is your imagination, o young people of art, thought and liberty. Become what you wish to be. Become the future!” He trumpets the last word like a cavalry charge, and then settles himself down to gaze at you smugly.

original: “An incisive enquiry,” rejoins the fanfir, stepping closer to let the hot liquid of his voice fill your ears. “Why indeed would a mutationist, who relies upon being able to palpably demonstrate his prowess, rely upon robotics? There are two reasons, [pc.short] Steele. The first, and this concern most fanfir share, is that a second pair of limbs rather impinges one’s ability to fly. In order for us to fly outside of our own world, you see, we need a second, anti-gravity harness. Whilst this synthetic aid may be easily shucked off and replaced with the other, a permanent set of arms places undue strain on it, and simply makes the business of flying clumsier besides. So the question is: do you sacrifice the occasional fleeting wonder that can be found in the air of alien worlds, the inexpressible delight of flight, for eternal manual dexterity?” He spreads his wings like a giant fan and bellows the answer at you. “No, no and NO!”

original: “Well, that one is much more simple,” Gene says, closing his wings and again stepping closer to you. There’s a clean metal sound and suddenly the synthetic harness unclasps, his arms falling to the ground. “I am a vain creature, it cannot be denied. And I have never encountered a second pair of arms that I felt would genuinely improve how I look. I mean, look.” The top half of his bulky outfit falls to the ground as well, and the fanfir swells his immense, red chest in front of you. “Really consider,” he insists, a grin of total confidence somewhere far above. “Would you really have a pair of spindly little arms sprouting out of this the entire time? Would you really ruin such a vista?”
